title: Author PROP-068 — agents-own-their-own-clones with unconditional pre-cycle blow-away; supersedes PROP-067 (which was misframed around cross-session sweep impossibility); audit all 13 scheduled agents for the same leak pattern
background:
PROP-067 (authored by tinker 2026-05-31T06:24Z in response to DIRECTIVE-20260531-003) confirmed that cross-session orphan sweep is structurally impossible (perms hypothesis (a) verified, /tmp option (ii) also ruled out via direct test). PROP-067 then recommended a (i)+(iv) hybrid: retire the dead sweep + add 2h same-session stale-clone recovery + document operator-side cleanup as the default maintenance pattern.

The operator rejected PROP-067 after surfacing the principle that 'an agent should always be able to clean up their own clones.' This reframes the problem entirely:
  - Cross-session orphans exist BECAUSE individual agents are leaking their own clones.
  - The right answer is not 'document operator-side cleanup'; that admits defeat. The right answer is 'each agent reliably cleans its own clone such that no orphan ever survives past the next cycle.'
  - PROP-067's 2h same-session recovery is partial: it requires the next cycle to actually run AND it allows up to 2h of orphan survival. A more robust pattern is unconditional pre-cycle blow-away with a short concurrency guard.

Root-cause hypothesis (likely same as PROP-066's silent-skip): workspace-sync.md is 1370 lines and the Cleanup section sits at the end. LLM context exhaustion / skip-by-omission / scheduled-task timeout / bash error mid-run leave $CLONE behind because the end-of-run rm -rf never executes. The bash EXIT trap at workspace-sync.md ~line 128 doesn't save us because the LLM finishing without running a final bash block means the trap never fires (no shell exit event).

Evidence: Steve has cleaned 3 stale dome-sync-clone directories this morning, all 10-15h old (post-fix-deployment of the now-dead cross-session sweep). The orphans are workspace-sync's own clones from prior runs that abandoned before reaching Cleanup. This is repeated operator-attention triggered by an in-agent bug — exactly the pattern PROP-068 should close.

The operator-stated principle (verbatim): 'an agent should always be able to clean up their own clones.'

what_changed_vs_prop_067.framing: Stop treating cross-session impossibility as the problem. The actual problem is that workspace-sync (and possibly other agents) abandon their own clone mid-run. Cross-session sweep was wrong because it tried to shift cleanup responsibility from each agent to a magic sweep that has no perms to do the job.
what_changed_vs_prop_067.ownership: Each agent owns cleanup of its own clone. Period. The cross-session sweep was an inversion of responsibility.
what_changed_vs_prop_067.operator_role: Operator-side cleanup becomes a bug-detection tool, not regular maintenance. If you find orphans, that's a signal that the owning agent has a bug to fix. The goal is zero recurring operator-side cleanup.

requested_work: Step 1 — Read PROP-067 + this directive's background and confirm understanding of the reframe. Then mark PROP-067 status='superseded' with superseded_reason pointing to PROP-068 (additive edit per the PROP-state-machine rules).

Step 2 — Diagnose the workspace-sync leak. Specifically: when workspace-sync's Cleanup section fails to run, WHY? Hypotheses to test: (a) LLM skip-by-omission (same as PROP-066's Step 4c — long prompt, Haiku skips the tail), (b) scheduled-task timeout terminates SIGKILL before Cleanup runs, (c) bash error in an earlier step aborts the run before reaching Cleanup, (d) MCP bash sandbox dies. Check workspace-sync run reports for the past 14 days, look for cycles that ended WITHOUT writing the post-run report → those are the leak events. Cross-reference against operator's observation of 3 orphans in the past ~24h to see if the run-report-missing count matches the orphan count.

Step 3 — Audit ALL 13 scheduled agents: poller, analyst, analyst-baby, curmudgeon, curmudgeon-verify, decider, integrity, tinker, social, sloppytoppy-score, sloppytoppy-rewrite, prune-integrity, workspace-sync. For each:
  (a) Does this agent clone? (Most do, per CLAUDE.md's 'each scheduled agent ... clone responsibly already' statement.)
  (b) What's the clone path naming convention? (workspace-sync uses 'dome-sync-clone'; others may use 'tinker-clone', 'decider-clone', etc.)
  (c) Does the agent have a pre-cycle blow-away pattern, or does it trust that prior cleanup happened?
  (d) Does the agent have a robust post-cycle Cleanup, or is it skip-by-omission vulnerable like workspace-sync?
  (e) Has the operator observed orphans for THIS agent? (Check operator-side perms — operator's shell can see all /sessions/<id>/.)
Produce a per-agent table in the PROP-068 audit section.

Step 4 — Design PROP-068 with the operator-stated principle as the foundation. Recommended approach (operator-suggested, but tinker should evaluate alternatives):
  (a) Unconditional pre-cycle blow-away: every cycle's FIRST action after the trap setup is `rm -rf $CLONE` regardless of mtime/age, then fresh clone. Cost ~30 sec extra per cycle (cloning anyway).
  (b) Short concurrency guard: before the blow-away, check if a pidfile exists indicating a concurrent run (rare — scheduled tasks are singleton — but cheap to check). Default 5-15 min mtime threshold IF pidfile is absent (the pidfile is the primary signal; mtime is the fallback).
  (c) Apply pattern to ALL 13 agents that clone, not just workspace-sync. Each agent's prompt gets the same boilerplate at the same location.
  (d) Consider extracting the boilerplate into a helper script (`monitor/scripts/agent-clone-init.sh` or .js) — mirrors PROP-066's architectural pattern of moving complex logic into helpers so prompts stay short and skip-resistant. Optional but recommended.
  (e) Strengthen post-cycle cleanup as belt-and-suspenders, not primary defense. Move it earlier in the prompt or extract to helper to make it skip-resistant.
  (f) Remove operator-side cleanup as a documented 'regular maintenance' pattern from CLAUDE.md (if it was added by PROP-067 — verify; if not yet added, just don't add it). Operator-side cleanup remains as a bug-investigation tool only.

Step 5 — Verification path:
  (a) Synthetic test: in a fresh sandbox, abandon a clone (write garbage state, kill the process), then run a new cycle, verify the abandoned clone is gone after Step 1's blow-away.
  (b) Real-world canary: 7-day observation window post-deployment. Operator should observe ZERO orphans in workspace-sync's session. If any other agent was found to leak in Step 3, observe zero orphans for those too. If orphans persist, that's a regression and we file PROP-069.
  (c) The cross-session sweep removal (PROP-067 P1, still valid) ensures we're not relying on the dead sweep as a safety net.

Step 6 — Output: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-068-agents-own-clone-cleanup.json with status='design-pending-operator-review' (operator wants to review the per-agent audit + the helper-script vs inline-boilerplate decision before authorizing implementation). Optional: include open_questions_for_operator for any architectural calls (e.g., helper script vs inline; pidfile path convention; pre-cycle blow-away log location).

Step 7 — Lifecycle: mark this directive completed with prop_id_authored='PROP-068'. Mark PROP-067 superseded as Step 1 noted. Do NOT auto-implement PROP-068 — that's a separate directive after operator review of the audit findings.
non_goals: Do NOT actually IMPLEMENT PROP-068 in this run — author only. Implementation is a follow-up directive after operator reviews the per-agent audit and chooses helper-script vs inline.
Do NOT touch PROP-066 implementation work (DIRECTIVE-20260531-004) — that's higher-priority and independent.
Do NOT add a system-level cron or root-equivalent cleanup mechanism — same non-goal as DIRECTIVE-003.
Do NOT add a 'sweep other agents' clones from workspace-sync' mechanism — same perms barrier as the dead cross-session sweep; same wrong-shape-of-fix.
Do NOT remove PROP-067 — mark it superseded, don't delete. Its diagnostic content (perms barrier proof) remains valuable historical record.

context_pointers.predecessor_directive: DIRECTIVE-20260531-003 (status: completed by tinker 2026-05-31T06:24Z, prop_id_authored=PROP-067 — that PROP is being superseded by THIS directive's output)
context_pointers.shared_root_cause_with_prop_066: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-066-step-4c-helper-script-extraction.json — the LLM-skip-by-omission failure mode that prevents Step 4c from running is hypothesized to also prevent the Cleanup section from running. If PROP-066's helper-script extraction pattern is the right fix there, it's likely also the right fix here.
context_pointers.claude_md_clone_discipline: CLAUDE.md 'Per-session clone discipline' section (operator-side rules added 2026-05-09) — pre-existing context on clone hygiene; PROP-068 should not contradict.
context_pointers.workspace_sync_cleanup_section: monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md ~line 1361 onward ('## Cleanup (mandatory, run last)') — the section that's failing to fire in leak cases.
context_pointers.exit_trap_location: monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md ~line 128 — the EXIT trap that's supposed to be the safety net but doesn't fire when LLM stops without a shell exit event.

expected_output: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-068-agents-own-clone-cleanup.json (status='design-pending-operator-review') containing: diagnosis from Step 2, per-agent audit table from Step 3, design from Step 4, verification from Step 5, and explicit open_questions_for_operator. PLUS: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-067-*.json with status flipped to 'superseded' + superseded_reason + superseded_by='PROP-068'.
estimated_effort: ~45-60 min Opus. Step 2 (diagnosis) and Step 3 (13-agent audit) are the bulk; design/output authoring is mechanical once the audit is complete.
priority_note: MEDIUM — same priority as the predecessor DIRECTIVE-003. Not a data-loss bug, just operator-side ~30 sec/week of cleanup that shouldn't exist. The principled framing matters: getting this RIGHT (agents own their cleanup) is structurally important because it prevents future 'shift responsibility to a magic sweep' anti-patterns from being proposed.
